data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-01T01:41:43.126Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.content:
Your code **doesn't use `Thread.Abort`**, `Thread.Interrupt`, or catch `ThreadAbortException`. It only uses `Thread.Start`, `Thread.Join`, and `Thread.Sleep` — all of which work identically in .NET 8. **No migration is needed** for Thread.Abort concerns.

That said, the code would benefit from modernization using `Task`/`Parallel.ForEach` instead of raw threads, and fixing the **captured loop variable** bug (`item` is captured by reference, so all threads may process the last item). But those are separate concerns from Thread.Abort migration.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating Thread.Abort usage in .NET Framework code to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort, Thread.Interrupt, or catch ThreadAbortException. It only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work identically in modern .NET.

Let me check the skill description for thread-abort-migration:
"DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


So the skill explicitly says not to use it for this case. I should tell the user directly that their code doesn't use Thread.Abort and needs no migration for that concern.
